topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box, labeled with a faded ‘Marketing’ sticker, sat accusingly in the corner of the new cubicle. Elara traced the edges with a fingertip, the cheap paper rough beneath her touch. It was the last tangible piece of the old office—a relic of shared coffee breaks and whispered lunchtime anxieties. She paused by the window, watching the familiar silhouette of the oak tree across the street, a tree she’d always pointed out to her old colleague, Mark. A dull ache settled in her chest, a heavy, familiar pressure. She picked up her new ergonomic mouse, the plastic cool against her palm, and forced herself to focus on the spreadsheet in front of her. But every time her eyes drifted, the scent of stale toner and old paper seemed to flood her senses, and she had to consciously blink away the lingering warmth.

[story 2]

“So, this is it,” Liam murmured, his voice barely audible over the hum of the new server rack. He leaned against the doorframe of the new department, his gaze fixed on the familiar hallway that had just become a memory. He clutched the worn leather folder containing his old project notes, the edges softened from years of handling. A tightness constricted his throat, making it difficult to swallow. He remembered the way the afternoon sun used to slant across his desk, illuminating dust motes dancing above his keyboard. He took a deep, shaky breath, the air feeling suddenly thin. He straightened his tie, a small, almost involuntary gesture of trying to reassert some old order in a world that was rapidly shifting.

[story 3]

Maria kept glancing at the framed photograph tucked discreetly behind her monitor—a group shot from the annual company picnic. The laughter in the picture seemed impossibly bright compared to the muted tones of her current workspace. She found herself smoothing the crease in the photo with her thumb, a repetitive, almost unconscious motion. A strange, watery feeling pricked at the corners of her eyes, and she quickly blinked them away, feeling a sudden urge to gather every stray piece of paper from her desk and fold them neatly, as if organizing the clutter could somehow organize the swirling uncertainty inside her. She picked up her phone, intending to call someone, but stopped, the weight of the unspoken goodbyes settling heavily on her shoulders.

Elara traced the edges with a fingertip, the cheap paper rough beneath her touch. It was the last tangible piece of the old office—a relic of shared coffee breaks and whispered lunchtime anxieties.
